text:
This study explores the reactive partitioning of cis and trans β-isoprene epoxydiols (IEPOX) onto non-acidified inorganic seed aerosols to better understand the mechanisms of isoprene-derived secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation. While no organic aerosol (OA) growth was observed on dry ammonium sulfate (AS) seeds, the introduction of IEPOX to hydrated AS seeds (liquid water content of 40–75% by mass) resulted in efficient and sustained OA production. This formation is identified as a kinetically limited process, where OA growth persists as long as a gas-phase IEPOX reservoir exists. No significant differences in aerosol yield or chemical composition were observed between the two IEPOX isomers. 

Notably, high OA loadings were maintained even when the seed particles were neutralized with NaOH, indicating that the reaction has a weak dependence on pH within the range typical of ammonium sulfate aerosols. In contrast, substituting ammonium (NH₄⁺) with sodium (Na⁺) completely inhibited OA formation after particle drying, suggesting that the ammonium ion may act as a critical catalyst or facilitate the reaction in the absence of strong acidity. Substitution of the sulfate (SO₄²⁻) anion with chloride (Cl⁻) resulted in a substantial reduction in OA mass, highlighting the importance of nucleophile strength in the ring-opening process. Using sodium chloride seeds, the Henry’s Law partitioning coefficient for IEPOX was determined to be approximately 3 × 10⁷ M atm⁻¹. 

Analytical measurements using aerosol mass spectrometry and filter-based UPLC/ESI-ToFMS identified oxygenated hydrocarbons, organosulfates, and tentatively, amines within the resulting OA. These findings suggest that IEPOX-derived SOA formation in the atmosphere may be limited by nucleophile availability rather than water or strong acid catalysts alone. This provides a laboratory basis for field observations where IEPOX-derived OA correlates weakly with particle acidity, emphasizing the role of ammonium and liquid water in driving isoprene chemistry.
